title: Policy and Applied Research
text:
This page lists selected applied projects and policy research with international institutions. The common thread is decision-focused analysis: measuring impact, identifying distributional effects, and turning complex empirical work into clear evidence for policy and strategy.

Current Work


Commitment to Equity Assessment for Pakistan
World Bank, forthcoming 2025
Ongoing CEQ work assessing the distributional incidence of taxes, transfers, and in-kind public spending in Pakistan, with a focus on poverty, inequality, and the redistributive performance of fiscal policy.


Commitment to Equity Assessment for Djibouti
World Bank, forthcoming 2025
Ongoing CEQ work extending fiscal-incidence analysis for Djibouti and evaluating the equity effects of taxes, transfers, and public spending across the income distribution.



Selected Published and Circulating Policy Work


The Role of Fiscal Policy in the Fight Against Poverty and Inequality in Djibouti
World Bank, 2023
Bilal Malaeb, Sandra N. Martinez Aguilar, Cristina L. Carrera Avila, Giorgio Maarraoui, Idriss O. Waais
This study evaluates the distributional impact of Djibouti’s fiscal system using the Commitment to Equity (CEQ) methodology. Results show that Djibouti’s fiscal policy is progressive, reducing inequality by roughly eight Gini points in 2017, with further gains achievable through expanded cash transfer programs. The paper combines rigorous incidence analysis with practical recommendations on targeting and fiscal design.
Read the report →



Towards a More Equitable Development: Constraints and Opportunities for Women in Djibouti
World Bank, 2023
Bilal Malaeb, Anne-Marie Duplantier, Giorgio Maarraoui, Éloïse Sophie Fluet
This gender assessment explores barriers to women’s economic participation in Djibouti, highlighting structural and legal constraints despite progress in education and rights. It combines quantitative and institutional analysis to identify where policy changes could improve participation, agency, and opportunity.
Read the report →



Willingness to Pay for Clean Air: Evidence from the United Kingdom
IMF Working Paper No. 23/35, 2023
Giorgio Maarraoui, Walid Marrouch, Faten Saliba, Ada Wossink
This paper estimates individuals’ willingness to pay for cleaner air in the UK using life satisfaction data and ordinal logit models. Exposure to NO₂, PM₁₀, and PM₂.₅ significantly lowers well-being, and the implied willingness to pay rises with education. The paper shows how behavioural and welfare metrics can be used to value non-market outcomes in policy design.
Download the paper →

title: Teaching Experience
text:
I teach across microeconomics, econometrics, and quantitative methods, with experience spanning undergraduate and MSc instruction, tutorials, seminars, and course design.

University of Manchester

Teaching Associate (2025 – 2026)
Intermediate Microeconomics · Mathematical Economics · Applied Microeconometrics (MSc) · Econometrics
Delivered lectures and teaching support in core economics and econometrics modules, and redesigned the MSc Applied Microeconometrics course to improve structure, empirical coherence, and student engagement.

Intermediate Microeconomics · Applied Microeconometrics (MSc) · Microeconomics I & II · Introduction to Mathematical Economics

title: Working Papers
text:
Selected research papers on climate, welfare, conflict, and behavioural adjustment in data-constrained environments.

Modelling Climate Shocks, Mobility Frictions, and Conflict in Farmer-Herder Economies
Lead paper.
This paper develops and calibrates a spatial general equilibrium model to quantify how climate change affects migration decisions, market adjustments, real incomes, and welfare for farmers and herders in Sub-Saharan Africa. It shows that climate damages are highly uneven across space, with particularly large losses in the Sahel, and that trade and migration partly cushion welfare losses while conflict intensifies. I use the model to evaluate climate-adaptation policies, showing that reducing frictions, especially trade costs, delivers broader and more balanced welfare gains than land expansion policies.
Draft available upon request.


Modelling Weather Shocks and Coping Mechanisms of Subsistence Farmers: Evidence from Nigeria
Working paper.
Using nationally representative household data for Nigeria matched to local weather realizations, this paper studies how subsistence farm households respond to both expected and experienced heat shocks during the growing season. I analyse three margins jointly: input allocation, crop choice, and planting timing. The results show that households adapt in forward-looking and reactive ways, but often through second-best adjustments driven by incomplete markets and subsistence constraints, with implications for productivity, resilience, and policy design.
Draft available upon request.


Climate Shocks and Conflict Transitions in Transhumant Africa
Working paper.
This paper studies how rainfall shocks in transhumant pastoral regions reshape the trajectory of farmer-herder conflict. Using high-resolution conflict, climate, and ethnographic data, I show that climate shocks matter differently across margins: they trigger first-time state-based violence in previously peaceful frontier areas, but do not explain renewed violence in locations with prior conflict histories. After onset, the same shocks help sustain early-stage conflict, with persistence amplified by hierarchical organisation and retaliatory norms. The results highlight the state-dependent nature of climate-conflict relationships.
Draft available upon request.

title: Economist | Applied Scientist
text:
PhD in Economics, University of Manchester
I am an economist and quantitative researcher with experience spanning causal inference, policy evaluation, market research, and applied data analytics. My work combines rigorous empirical design with large, messy real-world datasets to answer decision-relevant questions clearly and defensibly.
Alongside my PhD, I work with the World Bank Poverty & Equity Global Practice, where I contribute to Commitment to Equity (CEQ) assessments and broader fiscal-incidence analysis. Across academic and applied settings, I work on problems that involve behaviour, incentives, segmentation, distributional outcomes, and the effects of policy or market changes.





University of Manchester
Economist and Quantitative Researcher
World Bank
